questions.question: What is the main reason to reserve explicit wrap-up time in a system design interview?
options: It creates room for risks, observability, and trade-offs that otherwise get skipped.
It guarantees the interviewer will not ask follow-up questions.
It removes the need to estimate capacity early.
It lets you avoid discussing design weaknesses.
questions.explanation: The wrap-up is where senior judgment often shows up through trade-offs, failure thinking, and limitations.

questions.question: If your answer has no rough sizing by minute 15, what is the biggest likely problem?
options: The design may no longer be grounded in scale, cost, or bottleneck assumptions.
The interviewer will assume your API design is incorrect.
Your consistency model must become strongly consistent.
Your diagram will need more services to succeed.
questions.explanation: Without sizing, later component choices are harder to justify.

questions.question: Which pacing plan best fits a strong 45-minute interview default?
options: 3 minutes clarify, 2 minutes size, 30 minutes architecture, 10 minutes wrap-up.
6 minutes clarify, 6 minutes size, 12 minutes architecture, 14 minutes deep dive, 7 minutes wrap-up.
15 minutes clarify, 15 minutes size, 15 minutes wrap-up, no deep dive.
10 minutes API details, 10 minutes storage schema, 25 minutes database tuning.
questions.explanation: A balanced pacing plan reaches all critical stages without overinvesting too early.

questions.question: What is the best response when the deep dive is expanding beyond the time budget?
options: Add more detail quickly so the interviewer sees breadth.
Pause, restate the critical path, and defer lower-value subtopics.
Restart the architecture from the beginning with a cleaner diagram.
Skip failure modes because the deep dive is more important.
questions.explanation: Time control requires actively cutting lower-value detail.

questions.question: Which signal most directly reveals a pacing problem across repeated practice sessions?
options: Minutes spent in each stage and whether wrap-up was reached.
The number of infrastructure services named.
How many storage engines were compared.
The exact count of arrows in the system diagram.
questions.explanation: Stage timing shows where the answer routinely drifts.

questions.question: What is the core trade-off of a rigid time-boxing plan?
options: It improves data durability but weakens throughput.
It adds structure, but can sound mechanical if not adapted to the conversation.
It guarantees deep dives will never be necessary.
It prevents the use of back-of-the-envelope estimation.
questions.explanation: Good pacing is structured, but still flexible.

questions.question: If an interviewer interrupts at minute 8 with a scaling change, what should happen first?
options: Ignore the change until the deep dive stage.
Call out how the new constraint affects your current time plan and earliest architecture choices.
Replace every component with a managed cloud service immediately.
Move directly to observability because the design is now unstable.
questions.explanation: Adapting the plan and naming what changes is part of senior interview control.

questions.question: Which question best checks whether your wrap-up was strong enough?
options: Did I name the database twice for emphasis?
Did I summarize key risks, mitigations, and trade-offs before time expired?
Did I draw every cache layer in the request path?
Did I avoid making any assumptions at all?
questions.explanation: A strong wrap-up closes the loop on operational risk and decision quality.
